title: 怪屋
summary:
Sophia explains to Charles Haywood the internal dynamics of her "strange" and "cold-blooded" family. She defends Brenda Leoni, describing her as a proper lady who was likely attracted to Sophia's grandfather, Aristide, due to his charming and self-confident nature. Sophia characterizes her family members by various forms of coldness: her grandfather was a man who once stabbed two men in Smyrna without remorse; her grandmother was an arrogant woman who took responsibility for matters of life and death; her mother is a self-centered actress; Clementine, Roger's wife, is a ruthless scientist; Roger's uncle is kind but temperamental; and Sophia's father is excessively reserved. Sophia expresses a desire to quickly uncover the truth regarding Aristide's death.

Aunt Edith de Havilland, a woman in her seventies with a sharp gaze, joins them. She recognizes Charles and mentions his father. As they walk toward the house, Edith expresses her disdain for the police and discusses the reliability of old-fashioned maids. She reflects on the strangeness of Aristide being poisoned, noting that while she did not like him, his death leaves the house feeling empty. Edith reveals she has lived at the estate for forty years, having arrived after her sister Muriel married a "short, ugly foreigner." She explains that she stayed because of her love for the garden and because Philip Leonidis, a writer of obscure historical books, kept her there after his actress wife left him.
